source.quote: Every JSON-RPC message sent from the client MUST be a new HTTP POST request to the MCP endpoint.

requirements.exclusion: Recorded HTTP events carry status and conformance-relevant headers, not request methods or targets, so this clause's subject is invisible in a trace.

source.quote: The client MUST include an `Accept` header, listing both `application/json` and `text/event-stream` as supported content types.
The body of the POST request MUST be a single JSON-RPC request, notification, or response.
If the server accepts the input, the server MUST return HTTP status code 202 Accepted with no body.

requirements.exclusion: The trace schema records HTTP request and response observations as independent events without request/response correlation, so which response answered which POST is not decidable offline; the official suite exercises this live and the conformance gate runs it.

source.quote: If the server cannot accept the input, it MUST return an HTTP error status code (e.g., 400 Bad Request).

requirements.exclusion: Whether input was acceptable is server-internal judgment, and the trace schema records HTTP request and response observations as independent events without request/response correlation, so which response answered which POST is not decidable offline; the official suite exercises this live and the conformance gate runs it.

source.quote: If the input is a JSON-RPC request, the server MUST either return `Content-Type: text/event-stream`, to initiate an SSE stream, or `Content-Type: application/json`, to return one JSON object.
The client MUST support both these cases.

requirements.exclusion: What a client could have handled is client-internal capability; a trace shows the case that occurred, not the cases supported.

source.quote: The server SHOULD immediately send an SSE event consisting of an event ID and an empty `data` field in order to prime the client to reconnect (using that event ID as `Last-Event-ID`).

requirements.exclusion: SSE framing is consumed at capture: the tap reassembles `data:` lines into messages and drops event IDs and `retry` fields, so SSE-frame-level conduct is invisible in a trace.

source.quote: If the server does close the connection prior to terminating the SSE stream, it SHOULD send an SSE event with a standard `retry` field before closing the connection.

requirements.exclusion: SSE framing is consumed at capture: the tap reassembles `data:` lines into messages and drops event IDs and `retry` fields, so SSE-frame-level conduct is invisible in a trace.

source.quote: The client MUST respect the `retry` field, waiting the given number of milliseconds before attempting to reconnect.

requirements.exclusion: Checks may not consult time: trace timestamps are informational by design (determinism rule, 02-architecture.md), so timing conduct cannot be judged.

source.quote: The SSE stream SHOULD eventually include a JSON-RPC response for the JSON-RPC request sent in the POST body.

requirements.exclusion: Which HTTP stream delivered a message is not part of the trace vocabulary — events form a single capture-ordered sequence — so per-stream conduct cannot be judged from a recorded trace.

source.quote: These messages SHOULD relate to the originating client request.

requirements.exclusion: Relatedness is a semantic judgment over message content, and which HTTP stream delivered a message is not part of the trace vocabulary — events form a single capture-ordered sequence — so per-stream conduct cannot be judged from a recorded trace.

source.quote: After the JSON-RPC response has been sent, the server SHOULD terminate the SSE stream.

requirements.exclusion: Which HTTP stream delivered a message is not part of the trace vocabulary — events form a single capture-ordered sequence — so per-stream conduct cannot be judged from a recorded trace.

source.quote: Disconnection SHOULD NOT be interpreted as the client cancelling its request.

requirements.exclusion: How the server interpreted a disconnect is internal state; only conduct that follows from a misinterpretation could surface, and attributing it requires the interpretation.

source.quote: To cancel, the client SHOULD explicitly send an MCP `CancelledNotification`.

requirements.exclusion: A wish to cancel is client intent with no observable of its own; a session without a cancellation is not evidence one was wanted.

source.quote: The client MUST include an `Accept` header, listing `text/event-stream` as a supported content type.

The server MUST either return `Content-Type: text/event-stream` in response to this HTTP GET, or else return HTTP 405 Method Not Allowed, indicating that the server does not offer an SSE stream at this endpoint.
These messages SHOULD be unrelated to any concurrently-running JSON-RPC request from the client.

requirements.exclusion: Unrelatedness is a semantic judgment over message content, and which HTTP stream delivered a message is not part of the trace vocabulary — events form a single capture-ordered sequence — so per-stream conduct cannot be judged from a recorded trace.

source.quote: The server MUST NOT send a JSON-RPC response on the stream unless resuming a stream associated with a previous client request.

requirements.exclusion: Which HTTP stream delivered a message is not part of the trace vocabulary — events form a single capture-ordered sequence — so per-stream conduct cannot be judged from a recorded trace.

source.quote: The server MUST send each of its JSON-RPC messages on only one of the connected streams; that is, it MUST NOT broadcast the same message across multiple streams.

requirements.exclusion: Which HTTP stream delivered a message is not part of the trace vocabulary — events form a single capture-ordered sequence — so per-stream conduct cannot be judged from a recorded trace. A duplicated message in the capture order is also indistinguishable from a legitimately repeated notification.

source.quote: If present, the ID MUST be globally unique across all streams within that session—or all streams with that specific client, if session management is not in use.

requirements.exclusion: SSE framing is consumed at capture: the tap reassembles `data:` lines into messages and drops event IDs and `retry` fields, so SSE-frame-level conduct is invisible in a trace.

source.quote: Event IDs SHOULD encode sufficient information to identify the originating stream, enabling the server to correlate a `Last-Event-ID` to the correct stream.

requirements.exclusion: SSE framing is consumed at capture: the tap reassembles `data:` lines into messages and drops event IDs and `retry` fields, so SSE-frame-level conduct is invisible in a trace.

source.quote: If the client wishes to resume after a disconnection (whether due to network failure or server-initiated closure), it SHOULD issue an HTTP GET to the MCP endpoint, and include the `Last-Event-ID` header to indicate the last event ID it received.

requirements.exclusion: Conditional on client intent (the wish to resume), and the request method is not captured; when a resumption does occur its `last-event-id` header is recorded, but absence proves nothing.

source.quote: The server MUST NOT replay messages that would have been delivered on a different stream.

requirements.exclusion: Which HTTP stream delivered a message is not part of the trace vocabulary — events form a single capture-ordered sequence — so per-stream conduct cannot be judged from a recorded trace.

source.quote: Clients that no longer need a particular session (e.g., because the user is leaving the client application) SHOULD send an HTTP DELETE to the MCP endpoint with the `MCP-Session-Id` header, to explicitly terminate the session.

requirements.exclusion: Conditional on client intent (no longer needing the session), and the request method is not captured.

source.quote: The client MUST use HTTP POST to send JSON-RPC messages to the MCP endpoint.

requirements.exclusion: Restates TRAN-024's obligation as the section's first numbered step (two normative sentences, one constraint). Recorded HTTP events carry status and conformance-relevant headers, not request methods or targets, so the clause's subject is invisible in a trace.
